_comment: Optimized MCP server config — memory-limited, lazy, low-overhead. Generated by mcp_mem_audit.

_node_defaults._rationale: 128MB old-space cap prevents runaway heap; --expose-gc allows manual gc() between tool calls; 8MB semi-space reduces young-gen churn for short-lived tool buffers.

_python_defaults._rationale: Disable cyclic GC auto-run for short-lived request/response servers (manual gc.collect() between batches is cheaper); use libc malloc for predictable small-alloc behavior.

_optimizations: NODE_OPTIONS caps heap at 128MB (was unlimited, 24MB node_modules loaded eagerly)
--expose-gc enables manual gc() after spawn() child exits to reclaim stdout/stderr buffers
CTX_LOG_LEVEL=error silences debug/info logging that buffers in memory
CTX_NO_EMBED_INDEX=1 defers in-memory embedding index build until ctx_embed_stats explicitly called (saves ~50MB for large stores)
runCtx() accumulates stdout/stderr strings per spawn — these are short-lived but should be nulled after resolve. Recommend source patch: set stdout=null,stderr=null in finally block.
tools array (355 lines of schema) is built at module load — consider lazy const getter if startup memory matters
Heap capped at 96MB — this is a single-file 688KB bundled server (zod + ajv inlined), 96MB is ample for task board state
--expose-gc for periodic gc() after task file reads/writes
4MB semi-space — task planner does many small string ops, smaller semi-space = more frequent minor GC but lower peak
LOG_LEVEL=error suppresses verbose task-move logging
Bundled zod v4 + ajv are heavy (~40MB parsed) but unavoidable without rebuild; 96MB cap is the safety net
Recommend source-level: task markdown files are read into memory per operation — ensure file handles are closed and strings dereferenced after parse
Python server is lightweight (111 lines, stdlib only: subprocess/json/sys/os) — no heavy deps at startup
PYTHONGC=0 disables auto cyclic GC; this server is request/response with no long-lived cycles, manual gc.collect() between batches is cheaper
PYTHONMALLOC=malloc uses libc malloc for predictable small-alloc behavior (avoids pymalloc arena fragmentation)
PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 prevents stdout buffering from accumulating output in memory
subprocess.run() with timeout=10 is good — child is reaped, no zombie accumulation
Output truncated at 4000 chars (already implemented) — good memory bound
No state held between requests — excellent memory profile already
NOTE: -X dev enables debug assertions; remove for production if startup speed matters. Kept for safety.
NODE_OPTIONS caps npx-spawned node process at 128MB
PINECONE_LOG_LEVEL=error reduces log buffering
npx -y downloads package on first run then caches — subsequent runs use cache, no download memory spike
Pinecone client may buffer vector results in memory — 128MB cap is the safety net
Consider replacing npx with direct node path after first install to avoid npx resolver overhead (~20MB)
Go binary — not subject to NODE_OPTIONS. Go runtime has efficient GC by default.
WORST MEMORY RISK: root path is /Users/wws (entire home dir). Filesystem MCP may walk directory tree and cache file metadata.
RECOMMENDATION: Narrow allowed root to specific project dirs instead of entire /Users/wws to reduce directory cache memory:
args: ["/Users/wws/contestsystem", "/Users/wws/Qdrant_mysql_mlx_vector_engine"]
Go runtime GOGC=50 (more aggressive GC) can be set via env if memory pressure observed
GOMEMLIMIT=128MiB env var caps Go heap at 128MB (Go 1.19+ soft memory limit)
CURRENTLY DISABLED — good, saves ~47MB on disk + node process at runtime
Heap capped at 192MB (higher than others because mailparser + imap + express all loaded eagerly)
CRITICAL MEMORY LEAKS IDENTIFIED IN SOURCE:
1. this.transports = new Map() — SSE transports never cleaned up on disconnect (line 36)
2. this.validTokens = new Set() — OAuth tokens added but never expired/removed (line 40)
3. this.authCodes = new Map() — auth codes never purged after use/expiry (line 44, 1440)
4. express() + app.listen() starts HTTP server even in stdio mode — wastes ~30MB (line 1254, 1690)
5. simpleParser(buffer) holds full email body in memory during parse (line 957) — no size limit on buffer
6. imap connection not pooled — new Imap() per request (leaks sockets if not destroyed)
FIX RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Add TTL cleanup interval for authCodes (setInterval purge every 60s)
- Guard app.listen() with if (process.env.TRANSPORT_MODE === 'sse')
- Add maxBuffer size check before simpleParser (reject emails > 10MB)
- Null out transports map entry on SSE close event
- Use stdio transport only (TRANSPORT_MODE=stdio) to avoid express entirely
Eager imports: express, cors, dotenv, Imap, simpleParser all loaded at module top — lazy-load if possible
CURRENTLY DISABLED — Go binary, more memory-efficient than Node yahoo-mail server
GOMEMLIMIT=128MiB caps Go heap (Go 1.19+ soft memory limit)
GOGC=50 triggers GC when heap grows 50% (default 100%) — more aggressive for long-running server
Has connection pooling + singleflight (per CLAUDE.md) — good architecture, lower memory than Node version
RECOMMENDATION: Enable this instead of yahoo-mail (Node) if email MCP needed — Go uses ~30-50MB vs Node ~80-120MB
slog structured logging — set log level to error/warn to reduce log buffer memory
CURRENTLY DISABLED — good
If enabled: NODE_OPTIONS caps at 128MB
npx without -y will prompt — add -y to args for non-interactive
